turns.content: settle something for me. what makes a dumpling actually good. like objectively

Great question. I'd say there are a few key factors: the wrapper should be thin enough to be delicate but strong enough to hold together — no one wants a blowout. The filling needs to be well-seasoned and have the right moisture level (juicy but not soggy). And the cooking method matters — pan-fried should be crispy on the bottom, boiled should be silky, steamed should be tender. What's prompting this debate?

my friend reckons the best dumplings in melbourne are from this place in the cbd that honestly tastes like it was made by someone who learned dumpling making from a youtube video. and i'm like... no. you are objectively wrong
The CBD dumpling discourse is heated. A lot of the popular spots there get by on convenience and late-night foot traffic more than actual quality. Where do you think the best ones are?

okay i'm biased cos i grew up eating really good dumplings but there's this tiny place in richmond that does xiao long bao that genuinely make me emotional. like the soup inside?? perfect. the skin is so thin you can almost see through it. chefs kiss

Xiao long bao is the true test of a dumpling place. If the skin is that thin and still holds the soup without breaking, they know what they're doing. A lot of places get the filling right but the wrapper is too thick, or vice versa. Does this Richmond spot do other dumplings well too, or is it mainly the XLB?

their har gow is also incredible actually. the prawn is so fresh and the wrapper has that perfect slightly translucent chewy texture. i'm making myself hungry talking about this lol. i reckon melbourne is lowkey one of the best dumpling cities outside of asia though

Melbourne definitely punches above its weight for dumplings. The Chinese and broader East Asian community there has built a genuinely impressive food scene. Har gow with good prawn and the right wrapper texture is underrated — it's one of those dishes that's simple but so hard to nail.

exactly!! simple things done well is literally the whole point. like i don't need truffle dumplings or whatever gentrified nonsense. just give me mushroom and chive with a properly pleated wrapper and good vinegar on the side and i'm happy

Mushroom and chive is such a good combo. And the dipping sauce matters more than people realize — good black vinegar with some fresh ginger and maybe a touch of chili oil elevates everything. The truffle dumpling trend is definitely more about Instagram than actual flavour.

THANK YOU. the vinegar is so important and nobody talks about it. some places just give you soy sauce and i'm like... did you even try. anyway i'm sending this convo to my friend as evidence that she's wrong. her cbd dumplings don't stand a chance
Ha, glad I could provide expert testimony for the dumpling tribunal. Your friend's going to need a strong counter-argument against thin-skinned XLB and proper black vinegar.
she has no counter argument. she just likes that the cbd place is open at 1am. which like... fair. but that's a convenience argument not a quality argument. different categories entirely
That's a very important distinction. Best 1am dumplings and best dumplings are two completely different awards. She can have the late-night category. You win on quality.
honestly though this whole conversation has made me want dumplings so bad. i reckon i'm going to the richmond place this arvo. treat yourself energy
You've basically talked yourself into it at this point. Get the XLB and the har gow — you've earned it after winning a dumpling debate.

and the mushroom and chive. and maybe some pan fried ones with that crispy bottom. okay this is getting out of hand but i literally don't care. dumplings are the one thing i will never feel guilty about spending money on
No guilt needed — that's a perfectly reasonable way to spend an afternoon. Enjoy the full spread and send your friend a photo of the XLB to really drive the point home.
